just ok - These are ok... I am always looking for ways to make my Keurig more cost effective. These disposable filters are meant to be used with your "use your own coffee" inserts.  They do make it easy to pull out the grounds from your insert, but once they are wet, they edges are hard to grab up. Definitely wait until it has cooled a second or two before grabbing it. Also, it limits the amount of coffee you can actually put in the insert. After you start buying the inserts (plastic, stainless steel..etc), the cups for the inserts, etc...  I keep wondering if I should just go back to buying the Kcups.  It's still a bit cheaper, even with these paper liners so then I guess it comes down to if you want to do the 2 second kcup or spend a minute or two filling your insert.  A personal choice for everyone to make.

Excellent Quality! - This small stainless steel frothing jug is an excellent quality. It's sturdy. It's the perfect size for frothing milk for your coffee beverages. It's easy to clean and care for. I am very pleased with it.

Works Well - I am very happy to have found these! My husband and I have an Esperta2 machine. I've been happy with the prefilled pods that are available, but my husband prefers his whole bean coffee. This allows him the convenience of using the Esperta with his own coffee. These work well in our machine. You do have to make sure you line up the little hole correctly when you insert it into the machine. The directions explain how fine or coarse the coffee needs to be for using it. It comes with a spoon and brush. We used to have a Tassimo machine and there were not any reusuable pods that worked with it, so I am pleased that we found these. I'm excited to be able to use some nice flavored coffee beans. I love that these cut down on trash, too! **Update 5/21/2017 - I am still using these and still like them. However, I did want to add something to my review. They don't come out of the pod holder very easily. I usually have to turn the holder upside down and tap it slightly to get it to come out. When doing that, that sometimes dislodges the little filter screen. I didn't realize that it had come out one time and I threw it in the trash with the coffee grounds. Now that I know that can happen, I make sure that it's still there. And if it has come dislodged, I make sure to find it before I throw out the grounds. It's easy to put back in - it just snaps in place. I didn't realize it was even missing until I made coffee the next time with that pod and my coffee was full of grounds! Just wanted to share that.

French Press works better - I was so excited to use this for cold brew since I love so many OXO products, but this was a disappointment. I’ve been making cold brew in a French press until now, and I have been pleased with the results, but since it requires hands on filtering, I thought this OXO system would simplify the filtering and allow the machine to do most of the work. Wrong.
With a French Press, once the coffee has brewed, I can press the coffee and separate most of the grounds from the cold brew. Then I slowly pour it out into a mesh filter lined with a paper filter and wait for it to slowly seep through. I have to change the paper filter half way through before pouring the second half.
With this OXO system, you are supposed to be able to flip the switch and the cold brew will drip down in to the glass carafe below. But the grounds kept clogging the filter and then absolutely no liquid could get through. (I will also note that I have a quality conical burr grinder, so the grind should not be an issue.) I had to stand over it for about 15 min scraping the grounds to the side or scooping spoonfuls of grounds out of the brewing container. After 15 minutes of this, we were left with roughly 2/3 of the volume of cold brew we would have gotten from the same recipe in a French press. The reduced volume was partially due to me scooping grounds out of the way and throwing them in the sink, and partially because there was no way to effectively press the liquid out of the grounds. Also the taste was inferior. Again probably because the most concentrated cold brew couldn’t be pressed out of the grounds.
Overall the taste was nowhere as good and the process was more cumbersome than a French press. I wish I loved this machine, but on the plus side, it’s one less thing to sit on my counter and take up more space in the fridge.

I don't work for the company nor do I accept anything free or receive any compensation by saying this. Ask yourself these questions. I got this because after years of not drinking coffee but only occasionally - I didn't want to invest in a machine again. I had been off coffee for a few years but ran my k-type machine every few months for an occasional cup. I never got much value from the k machine for last few years. I cleaned it religiously with vinegar of commercial cleaner but all three machines I had failed pretty quickly. When this last one died I said enough. I had not been happy with the lukewarm result from all of them. I like my coffee (when I have it) strong and HOT. BUT all three machines I had tried produced barely more than room temp beverage.

So, I knew you could open the cups and use with a filter with boiling water over a mug. And I was surprised at the great cup of hot coffee I got from a kcup that was ages old even. So I got new coffee in can and then purchased this. At first it was so so with the filters from my old mr coffee even (I actually use those filters for making ghee) and I finally broke down and purchased proper pour over filters for it and what a difference. This is a nice product and I can only imagine what it is like with fresh ground beans but my coffee drinking is still sporadic and not worth that kind of grinding effort.

Am fine with these not sure how much longer using pods - First, I did not have the problem where I ordered this many and got only a portion. They appear to have corrected the UPC problem. My last batch of these, I was not thrilled with some sealing problems (I use the black perfect pod appliance to make pods). But this batch seems OK.

I just don't know how much longer I want to have to make my coffee pods. When I had the time to custom grinds beans to put into these things it made a decent cup. But now I am struggling among various espresso products to fill these things with the right amount that I get coffee, not dark water. You know what I mean? It is a lot trouble for what doesn't seem to be worth it anymore.

Once the sumatra pods I used to get became unavailable it was a toss up whether I would continue my relationship with my Senseo. I am looking at the Keurig grrrrrr because I don't want to have to do this anymore but I want a whole cup of coffee.

YSo, while this product is adequate. And the perfect pod maker whatever is still making, your mileage may vary.

Agree w/several others - too much trouble to go to for brown water - I have my Senseo almost a year (just found out is being recalled - please check yours! [...] ). Anyway before finding out about the recall I started having trouble finding the only coffee pod I like, Sumatra. It was no longer in any grocery or other retail store in area and even Walmart shiptostore no longer carried. Tried every other non-flavored pod I could get my hands on locally and none were acceptable. There are still places on the web but didn't want to spend even more for what is expensive coffee already. Figured I would try this and would use espresso fine grind as recommended since I like a strong cup. Well, I tried both regular espresso grind and a very fine grind. Both expensive enough that it wouldn't be much cheaper than the packaged pods even. Tried one cup (with an empty just water run first to get everything hot), one-cup twice for a mug full, and two cup setting. The coffee varied from mediocre to impossible to drink. For the most part, I get brown water with a foaminess (not foam on top, more a "general" foaminess) You can tell when it is coming out, after first blast, it is hardly colored anymore. And no coffee aroma. The best results are packing as tightly as possible with the finest grind and using one cup setting (running without pod first to heat everything up and having fresh water in it rather than what was sitting in boiler since yesterday).

That result, however drinkable, is not worth the mess. And what a mess. I knew the pods would be a little messy to fill and figured it would be the price to pay for this being cheaper than buying pods on the web. But the mess to empty! Yikes. Now I remember why I got the Senseo in the first place. To not go through the mess of grinds to deal with. And very fine espresso grinds packed tightly into the pods are very messy indeed to clean out. More like dirt. I don't have a garbage disposal so this is awful. Not only do I have the dry grounds mess filling, I have the wet mess disposal to deal with.

For now I have bought the Sumatra on the web. And will use that until the replacement machine comes. Since that machine is black (that is all they are giving in exchange for recalled machines) and mine is red (the other reason I bought it!). I think I will sell the black one unused and get myself a Keurig. Senseo is too much trouble and whether the Sumatra pods have become difficult to find because they are maybe closing up? Or if it is just being phased out, I can't find a coffee I like in their line anymore so why go on with this.

If you are into very watered down coffee, the ecopads are an option for you. To me it is too much mess for nothing.

text: Broan QS130 - Unit arrived well packaged and protected all the way to Alaska. Unit does not ship with any bulbs,wiring or charcoal filters (if you intend a non-vented installation). The fan housing was crooked and the plastic fan had a wobble that struck on one side. A little dead blow hammering and pry bar and I was able to straighten the defect. The fan motor mount was clearly shorter on one side. Appears to be manufacturing defect vs shipping. No external damage noted when unpacking and this is in the heart of the beast.. Had it not been for this I might have rated it 5 star...  Having to purchase additional filters and bulbs just to make it work was a little unreasonable given the premium price of the unit. Good value - Was able to use for my machine, which is a Farberware single cup. Taking the kcup holder and putting this in with the ground brew, I did not see any water coming out from around it. * Definitely do not fill above the mesh!

Not sure about this version of the prodcut - I've been using Keurig official ones for a long time. On this package, one of the screens ripped off the plastic rim after less than a month of normal, daily use. This has never happened to me before; normally they get so caked with old coffee that I throw them out and replace. The second one in this package has lived up to expectations however.
Five Stars - Best one I've tried.
Five Stars - Works great
Got it quick Hard to find - Works great
Perfect fit and performance - Zero coffee grounds in the bottom of the pot. Great tasting (ground) coffee bean flavor.
Good strudy filter - Seems like a very good value to me
Filters - Filters fit perfectly - clean easily - last through 12-15 uses without a problem.  Be sure to dry thoroughly before storing between use.  I use 1-2 times weekly.

My-Kap - This is one of the best purchases I have ever made.  I have not found a K-cup that equals the quality and aroma of my favorite brands of coffee.  I now enjoy the instant service of my Keurig and a GREAT cup of coffee to start my day with a smile and "ummmmmm so good".  I find that a really good fine grind works best - I use a coffee measure and get a perfect, groundless mug each time- I like a good strong cup with good aroma.  My-Kap service was great - prompt, efficient and no over-packaging.  I have wasted money buying the Keurig re-usable filter, does not make good coffee because of water flow, does not fit in K-cup carousel tower. I also tried Solofill, it gave off lots of residue in bottom of mug, coffee had a bitter taste -- I am so happy I tried one more time and this time My-Kap was a WINNER all the way around, especially nice not to go through removing the K-cup housing each time. I also re-use the K-cup filter, I just rinse it well after each use and let it dry - no need to order filters or make your own, the old intact filter works great and I can store several in my carousel. Better coffee, saves money, great buy.
Four Stars - It took some time to get these filters but they work great
Five Stars - It fits perfect in my coffee maker

Clean up your coffee - If you use the reusable pods but don't like the "mud" that the plain screen let through you need to get these. Work great also easier to clean, you don't have to beat the used coffee out of the filter it lifts out easily.
Got to have these - Love these much better value than buying pods.Dont waste coffee by pouring half a pot down the sink

seasoned cold brewer here, lots of info below, brewer works great, easy to use - I have been drinking "cold brew" for well over a decade now. The occasional coffee house would have a cold brew system, and so it was a treat back then to purchase a cup of extra strong and extra smooth coffee. Then I started making it at home. There were no special brew systems yet available. So I would simply take a big pitcher dump a bunch of ground coffee into it, then top it off with water. Cover, let sit for a full day, then either carefully pour into a cup as desired (while trying to keep grounds from falling out), or when I was feeling more motivated I would strain through a cheesecloth. Either way, I had easy cold brewed coffee. YUM.

Forward to the past several years where cold brew coffee has hit critical mass. Most everyone has heard of it, most coffee shops offer it, and many stores sell it. Many cold brew systems are available, just in different styles. Some coffee pot systems are trying to state that they offer cold brew option- but be aware-- cold brew is NOT ICED COFFEE. It is only cold/room temp water on ground coffee, there is no heat applied at any point (save for if you decide to heat it up after the fact). Ok, so.. this brewer:

I have tried a few different types of cold brew systems since my days of dumping loose ground coffee in a pitcher. These days things are a little more stream lined and a tad easier. None of them do anything extra special, it is really just about your preference. It basically breaks down to needing a filter of some sort (can be resuable metal cylinder or paper, like this one has), a vessel to "brew" in. That's it. This system happens to have a little extra in its design. I thought it might be a little much, but I found it pleasant to use, and there was no mess.

There appears to be many parts, but it is super easy to figure out. Instructions are super easy to understand if need be.

Steps:

--The brew container* (see additional info on this part below) is what what you will use to "brew" your coffee. The ratio is 1:8? 8 ounces (1/2 lb) of coffee to about 64 ounces of cold or room temp water. This does not need to be exact. You will not mess up a batch of cold brew if you use a little too much or a little less (though more would be better since you can water it down later if need be). You put course ground coffee into the included paper filter. If not already in base, then place coffee filled bag in side. Put cover on, and set aside on counter or in fridge for 12-24 hours. I usually wait a full day. Sometimes I go longer depending on when I started it. In this case, it was late afternoon, and I kept it on counter for a full day and a half so I could drink some in the morning.

*1. the brew vessel has a lever and stopper you will need to attach. Super quick and easy. Make sure the level is in the stop/closed position before adding water.
*2. Also, there is a 2 pack of carafe filters (small round sponges) that are optional use. I find they are easy to rinse and reuse.

--Once you are done "brewing", put the brewing vessel on top of the grooved pitcher. This is where the lever on the brewing vessel comes in. Push it open. This immediately drains your cold brew into a pourable pitcher. Once the brew vessel is empty, take out bag full of spent coffee and toss it out (OR if you are super thrifty and careful.. perhaps rinse and reuse?). Your pitcher is now full of delicious, smooth, concentrated coffee. The pitcher comes with a lid. The pitcher is of a workable size- easy to hold, small enough to store in fridge, yet holds nearly 2 liters. The size is perfect for storing in the fridge. Indents in the pitcher make it easy to grab/hold.

EDIT: Unlike other brew systems, it dawned on me that I could get another batch "brewing" at any time after the first one. This is because the brew container is separate from the storage container. My other "brewer" is a brewer and pitcher in one. So not until I finish the container (or find a different container to store concentrate in) can I make another batch. With this system, I can simply start another even if storage pitcher is in use (so long as I can access it it after brewing, which I tend to brew for 24-36 hours). Anyway, thought I would come back to add that in since it wasn't an immediate thought when I was using this initially.

"Cold brew" is considered a coffee concentrate. The idea is to have a strong coffee that can withstand being watered down by ice and other coffee additions (cream, syrups, etc) without losing the coffee taste. Or you can drink it straight if you like very strong coffee (I do!). In addition to that, it makes a very smooth coffee. Usually reduces/removes acidity. And lastly, although it seems like it is only consumed cold, it technically can be warmed up to be enjoyed hot. Some people cold brew to reduce the acidity, and then want to still have warm/hot coffee. Go for it. For me, I just really like the added oomph of taste that cold brew coffee makes. I have even noticed that it can make kinda blah coffee even better by being cold brewed. (have you ever purchased a container of coffee and not really liked it? Try cold brewing it-- it just might change things enough to make it palatable!).

Specially about this system: In short, I like it. I first thought it was going to be too many parts to deal with, but I see that the parts it comes with that once they are on, no need to take them off (save for if your brewer needs extra TLC when cleaning). The brew vessel AND a pitcher is more than what is really needed (often I see that a vessel could be the pitcher just by taking bag/filter out)- but this makes it super clean and easy to deal with. I DO like the paper filter over a metal filter in terms of speed of clean up, but some people would prefer not to have excess waste.

This style of system is really going to come down to preference. All the parts on mine look good, work soundly, and I have no problem with using this. With one caveat, the pitcher fits into the brew vessel for easy, compact storage (put lid on pitcher, but find spot for vessel lid). This system comes with TWO PLA bag filters, so you will need more. I do not know what this system costs but it doesn't need to be expensive to be effective. I see that the 25 pack of the filters is $15 (or less if buying 25 pack of regular filters). I have a glass with metal filter system that works well for around $25 although makes half as much at a time). Or just use my old school method of a pitcher with loose coffee in it. :)

If you choose this, know that it absolutely makes a great pitcher of cold brew coffee concentrate. No problems there.

Because I do not know what the price is, I can't automatically recommend this- but I would nudge people in the direction of this style. I personally think it is even easier than the metal filter style, and this makes a decent amount- not too much, but not too little. If it is within reason of other brewers, I would be real pleased. While I normally don't take into consideration price, I do if there are enough of similar products on market-- and knowing that cold brew systems are pretty much all the same except for filter material- that I can comfortably comment on it. Therefor I would normally ask, why would I recommend this over other brewers? That is when price might come into play, along with some other design features. Since this also requires use of paper filter, that is an additional cost. This just comes with two filters, so you would pretty much need to add in the additional cost right away. Say, if I was ordering this I would also order a pack of filters at same time.

What would improve upon product: While the pitcher and lid fits into the brewing vessel for more compact storage, the lid of the brewing vessel has nowhere to go. It could be placed on bottom so all of it just sits on it, or it can be placed on top but there again, it is just resting on it. It doesn't make for perfectly tidy storage. It would work if the brew vessel was taller or the pitcher was shorter-- then the pitcher would nestle down into the brew vessel and the lid could just go on top. The difference doesn't seem like it would be enough to displace water/coffee ratio, but would certainly add to the tidiness of storing it.

Current rating: 5 stars. It totally works as intended, and I have used enough cold brewers of various styles to be extra pleased with this one. I really didn't think it would be better than my simple glass pitcher with metal filter, but the paper filter and ease of separating the brewed coffee into a separate pitcher has impressed me. This system may be my new go-to brewer and I will pass my other one on to a friend. This is a matter of preference for me. Ultimately the rating goes toward the fact that it works well and produces a solid "cold brew" end result. I have been drinking a cup (ok, maybe 3) while writing this so excuse my length. I feel like writing a dissertation on cold brew while simultaneously running full speed on my treadmill. WEEEEEEEEEEe cold brew is fun! :)

perfectly fine unbleached compostable coffee filter - "If You Care" products are pretty good. I buy them when I can as I like that they are all unbleached and chlorine free.

The #4 coffee filters work fine in a typical brew system or in use with a pour over style. I had used it in the latter and found that if I pressed the filter with my hand to squeeze out the last of the liquid from the coffee grounds, that it could tear along the seam. I don't think most people would encounter this as most people would use this for coffee to drip into using a machine. But just keep it in mind if you have a need to hand pour hot water over ground coffee- just let gravity do its job, and don't bother to help it along by squeezing out the rest.

It is compostable! After you make your coffee, toss it all into your compost pile!

They seem to make their products with care. Whenever possible I select one of their products over the typical beached or plastic laden product. I am not at all perfect in how I treat my environment, so even if I could do a lot better, I like knowing that t here are companies creating products that help us to have the "Least possible environmental impact" in our day to day lives.

Perfectly fine product, does the job with care - Every "If You Care" product I have used has been a good product. I am particularly keen on their parchment paper for baking. Unbleached, chlorine free, and better for the environment. As for this product, this is my second package of No 2 Coffee filters, 100 ct. I don't go through them too often, but I use this size when I am brewing a cup of coffee using a collapsible single cup coffee maker (typically used for hotel stays and camping, but occasionally in house too). I think typical coffee makers use No 4. Grounds never seep through it, and my coffee always tastes great. I have no use for the typical white paper filters, and so I am happy to be able to buy this product here as well as in stores. I have zero complaints or concerns, so a full five star rating is warranted.

High quality, stainless steel, reusable k-cups, not for the Supreme models... - I'm sure these are perfectly fine reusable k cups. They are stainless steel and high quality. The problem is I have the K cup supreme machine from Costco. LOL! The supreme uses cups with 5 holes. So please pay attention to what model you have. I'm sure these are 5-Stars.

A complete dryer vent cleaning kit that all homeowners should have... - A few yrs ago, our dryer wasn't drying anymore. We reached into the vent that goes out the house, and holy moly guacamole! It was completely stuffed with lint. The previous homeowner probably never cleared their dryer vents. We make it a point to clean it from time to time now, and this kit is going to be very helpful. The 30 ft vent brush is going to be very useful. We've been cleaning with just our hands and some broom sticks, and that's been working fine. We don't technically need this kit, but how can you rate this kit anything  but 5-Stars? It has to be better than broom sticks, right? LOL! 5-Stars.

Nice solid 4 ft x 10 ft foil duct... - The most economical foil duct at your local HD might be the Everbilt brand that is 4 ft x 8 ft long. They also have a 4 ft x 25 ft if you need something longer, but for a dryer vent, 10 ft is more than enough. This looks heavy duty, the clamps feel very strong. We'll see how this works. Looking forward to installing this. 5-stars!
Handy - It is a good scoop. You just have to watch how much you fill it.

Great Set but LOVE the filters! - I found these worked just as well as my other reusable k-cups and I love the filters so much. I don't find that it changes the taste of my coffee at all. The filters make it super easy to clean my reusable cups with just simply throwing out or composting the grounds and filter; rinse the k-cup and you're done. So much easier than using them without filters. As far as the price of this, well if you bought everything separately then it would be a little more expensive, however this is a one time purchase kit because I wouldn't want to have to buy more reusable kcups just to get the filters that I can get from someone else. Either way though, I am happy with this set and the kcups work great and hold up perfect for making coffee ahead of time to have when ready to use them. Great set.

Great for the Week - These are great and work just as they should. I love to fill them for the week so I don't have to waste so many other kcups. These are great and so far I've had no problems at all with these.
Great - A nice, robust coffee flavor. It works great in my Keurig brewer. Makes a great cup of coffee. I do recommend it.

Works great! - This universal filter for use in Keurig coffee machines is great. It allows me to use my favorite coffee from a local roaster, without having to use K-cups. I also like that there is no plastic cup to add to the local dump. I had a little bit of an issue getting it to work at first. I actually called Keurig and a representative talked me through the process of removing one of the machine parts and replacing it with the sleeve that comes with the filter. That process could have been made a little bit more clear in the written instructions that came with the filters, but the representative was very patient and helped me all the way until I had it working properly. Yeah! It works great. I highly recommend getting the REAL Keurig filters and not one of the knock-offs out there. I've tried a few of those and couldn't get some of them to work properly. You may spend a little more on the real Keurigs, but I think it is worth it. They are made better and work properly with the machine.

Nice towels - I am happy with these. The absorbency was good. I liked the bright colors. Be careful when you wash them with something else if you get the colored ones because they can leak. I am happy with the size of them. They are very versatile - I use them for a wide variety of things like counters. They are pretty soft. They do a good job, and I would recommend them.
Excellent value - A very well made filter basket at a great price.

Epic Fail - This is the third one of these I've tried. None of them fit the way they say and none of them work. Can't return it without a fee.  Spews hot coffee out all over the place. Lid blows off and there is a terrible mess. I've had Keurigs since they first came out. I have tried every version of this filter they make. Not a one has ever worked successfully.
Fit perfectly - Fit perfectly in my Aicok single coffee maker. They are a little easier to clean if you let the coffee grounds dry out a little then dump them.

money saving and eco friendly - This is a very convenient way to use the Keurig more economically and use your favourite brand of coffee. The filters are a little hard to get out at first, until you get the hang of it, but after that it becomes second nature. The pot itself is sturdy and holds up well to the abuse of being used many times. The lid is removable for easy cleaning. This product made life less expensive and easier, so yep, I'd recommend it to other shoppers. You should be careful when removing it when it is full of coffee grounds though, not to pull it out by the lid, as it can fall off and spill hot grounds. Otherwise, great.

Works perfectly - This is a great little frothing pitcher. No issues with cleaning it up, it comes out sparkling every time and the contour is perfect so when you pour it doesn't make a mess. Bought this for use with our cappuccino maker and have been very pleased with it. It is deep enough that it hold enough for more than one drink at a time as well, so that is nice. For the money, this is a great purchase.
Work Great! - I bought these for my hubby as he doesnt like flavored coffee but loves the Keurig. They work great, easy to clean, and no pollution from throw aways and he can use his favorite coffee.

product_name: Shark Vertex Pro Powered Lift-Away Vacuum
text: Works For My Magic Chef Portable Dryer - I bought these to replace my filters for my Magic Chef portable dryer and they work well. No complaints.

Easy and no waste. - This summer I have become dependent on cold brew coffee. I was just putting the grounds in a jar with water, and then 24 hours later filtering through a sieve and a coffee filter - like a savage! This lets me use the same jar, but I just yoink the Rumble Go out of the jar the next day. No more panicking about making sure that I have enough filters. And the Rumble Go empties easy into the compost bucket - very green. The Rumble Go will be paired with my Nalgene bottle for glamping this month. I may not have an expensive coffee house budget - but I have very fancy coffees!
I make my cold coffee with a tsp of vanilla, a tsp of stevia and a glug of good oatmilk.

The easiest way to make good coffee anywhere - This is the 5th one I've bought - all the previous ones were nicked by my friends or I gave them away. Look, good coffee anywhere is not difficult. You pack this, and some filters in your kit. If you are going somewhere with good coffee, get it there, if not, put some nice ground coffee in a bag and add that to your kit. If you will be somewhere with mugs, use those, if not, add a mug to your kit. So if you will be on a ship crossing the Pacific with only instant coffee on board for 4 weeks you pack this doohicky, about 30 filters, enough ground coffee for 30 days, your favorite mug, and you're set! Easier to clean than a french press, far less breakable too.  I just added a kit to my tiny travel trailer. Just say NO to coffee pods!
Quick service - The item arrived quickly and was exactly as described. I've used this coffee cone on several trips and at work when the coffee maker broke. It will make 1-6 cups of coffee perfectly.
Great - Excellent value, works well.

works great - no real issues - I've used it on one trip so far. It has a couple obvious drawbacks, but it works every bit as well as I hoped. I had to try a couple times to figure out the best way to fold it back up.

Perfect. - I'm not going to try any other filter. I love the coffee I make with these. I roast my own at home and use the Hario V60. These fit perfectly and make good coffee. Now there is only one coffee house in town that can lure me with better stuff than what I can make at home.
Hard to find this six - I use these with a single serve (non pod) travel coffee maker. Perfect for daily or travel, makes clean up easy.
Five Stars - Work well with my coffee toddy.

Great filters and an even better price - I bought these for home use. I shouldn't have to buy filters again for at least the next five years.
They are a good quality filter.
Luke  warm tea - This coffee maker is beautiful, but it’s makes Luke warm tea with a weak flavor.

Can't figure why this will not fit! - The Keurig that I brought from Amazon is this one: It is does not have a number to it. It is fairly new.
Keurig K-Classic Coffee Maker, Single Serve K-Cup Pod Coffee Brewer, 6 to 10 oz. Brew Sizes, Rhubarb

But this will not fit. I tried every which way but loose and it will not fit! What is that about. What I own is a standard Keurig, nothing high tech, just a Keurig.

The two Pods are nice, bright, and sure to hold your coffee grounds. There is a handy lid and little handle. But, I can't figure it out!

Exact match to Keurig filter. - Matched perfectly to the OEM filter. It fit in my Keurig k-155 with no issues. The filter material is similar texture and thickness to OEM. Each filter comes individually wrapped as well.

I have had no issues with deterioration of filter material or charcoal escapement since I have been using. Overall, these are a great affordable alternative to the OEM.

In the pictures the one on the left is Keurig's and subject filter is on the right.

product_name: KEEPOW Reusable Cone Coffee Filters #4 (2-pack), 8-12 Cup Gold Permanent Coffee Filter for Cuisinart
text: Decent Quality - The plastic portions of these reusable filters are a bit flimsy, like the handle, but they work well for as long as they'll last.
As Advertised - This duct is standard, and comes at a standard price. Fairly well-made and should hold up well.

Outstanding - This is a great addition to any kitchen, especially if you don't have an ice-maker in the freezer. Setting it up is simple, as is the initial cleaning (it mostly self-cleans). The ice it produces is perfect: smaller, cylindrical cubes that are not overly-solid. It is like the ice you would find at Sonic and some other fast food places. It makes ice quickly and always keeps the drawer full. It's just great.

Save Some Money - This is so easy to use, and the results I've gotten have been great! You're creating cold brew concentrate that you water down before adding ice and drinking. Once you find the right balance, depending on which coffee you're using, it makes for great-tasting cold brew. This will save you a TON of money if you're always running to Starbucks or Dunkin for cold brew. Highly recommended!
To thick to fit in a pod - It is too thick to fit in a pod.
Best K-Caraf filter that I have used so far - It is much better than other brands that I have used in terms of being thin enough to fit easily in the mesh pod and does not impede cap closure.

Not for Keureg 2.0 K550 - I have a Keureg 2.0 K550. Doesn't work with the filters. I tried several times and it overflows or I got the pressure error. Probably giving to my father in law since his 1.0 can use the filters.

Good quality, don't overfill, don't use filter. - Cups work well, but make sure you don't overfill them. My other ones had more mesh to release the water and these have less. Don't use filters for easier cleaning, they overfill and make a mess or you get the pressure error. Otherwise they are not bad.

I have tried the other purple ones that have more mesh but the quality was not as a good as these. I may try the gold mesh next time to see if they are any better once these wear out.
So hard to find large tea cases like this.  Nice and roomy for larger pitchers - A screw or some type of mechanism to keep the cap on would have been great.

Excellent large tea/coffee "bag".  A little smaller than a normal sized man's fist. - The lid does not attach however.  But since there are two protruding lips on the body, a simple rubber band will firmly hold it in place (if you'd like to submerge this into a cooler like I wanted to).
Good buy - This complements the metal pods. It does exactly what it says. Sturdy and durable.

Better than plastic - I've tried the plastic refillable k-cup pods and they are okay but these are way better. They don't leave any off taste, they are easy to use and clean up easily. I don't use a keurig that often, but when I travel, it's easier to take these and make my own from freshly ground coffee beans. That way I get coffee I like the taste of and the ease of not having to go to a coffee shop for a decent cup.

My coffee and the Keurig - I was tired of using substandard coffee in my keurig so ordered these and now I can use my Kona beans in my easy to use drip coffee maker. They are easy to use and easy to fill and easy to clean. Finding the right grind took a try or two but once I had it now I can get a decent cup of coffee that perfect for my tastes.

Cute and useful - I originally got this as a frothing pitcher but find it works just as well as a placeholder under my coffee machine. I keep it right under the spout and it collects the coffee that I can then pour into my cup. Sturdy and useful. I just rinse it out and then put it back and it’s ready for tomorrow. Gives me a couple of minutes to get my drinking mug ready and when I’m ready the coffee is ready. I expect I could use it for something else, but I’m perfectly happy with it right where it is.

Works? - I had trouble in the beginning with this since you have to have the correct grind and fill the capsule to the right level but once I got it, I have more positives than failures. It does work fine and now I can make my own capsules to get the flavor I want. I’d obviously prefer to be able to purchase the “perfect” capsules but since I haven’t found any I like better than my freshly roasted and ground beans, I’ll put in the time for that perfect cup. It’s pretty easy once you get the grind and level right.

More trouble than I want to invest - These take a lot of work to make happen. I was hoping to be able to “make” my own capsules but you have to be sure to grind the coffee fine enough (and then sometimes it still didn’t deliver the results I expected) you also have to use a previous capsules foil which has the code on it. Just these made the whole process more time consuming than I wanted. I tried a few, now I’m onto testing various types of existing capsules to find my “perfect” blend. That’s me..if having your own custom capsules are worth it and you want to put in the time and effort, this might be worth a try.

Nicely shaped, no drips - I generally use this as a catch for my Jura to rinse into unless I’m making cappuccino (which isn’t a frequent occurrence). This is nicely shaped, doesn’t take a ton of extra room, fits easily under the spouts and doubles as a frothing pitcher when needed. Very sturdy, no drips. Love it!

Arrived in the nick of time - My last frothing pitcher had just died when this arrived so it went to work right away. It's easy to use, works perfectly and goes into the dishwasher. I also use it to make single cup of coffee or tea in and then I move it to my cup. Smooth and sleek it does what it's supposed to and looks good doing it.

Fits Philips 3221/44 Espresso Machine- So Far So Good - We have a Philips Espresso machine that the entire family loves. It makes fantastic coffee of all sorts. Buying the filters can get expensive, especially when the unit is not being used for a period of time. I wanted to try this as an alternative.

IS IT THIS THE SAME?
This is just like the filter that comes from Philips for the 3221 machine. I think that the number is CA6903/10. It is not identical, but it is very close.

PERFORMANCE
So far this performs like the other filters, but the true test will be over a few months to see if we get the same long term performance. For now, it is good and fits well and seems to perform similar to the others we have from Philips.

THE BOTTOM LINE
This seems to fit and work like the filters from Philips. If this has the same long term performance, then we will switch to using these.

MY RATING
FIVE STARS. So far that is my rating. If anything changes, I will update this review and rating to let you all know.

Perfect If You Don't Like The Water Speeding Through Your Aeropress - I have had an Aeropress for some time now, and one of the reasons I stopped using it is because the hot water speeds through the Aeropress so fast no matter how fine I ground it that coffee was too weak. I even used a few different metal filters. This fixes that.

WHAT IS THIS LIKE?
This is a combination of a cap that has a pressure sensitive silicone release valve set into the middle of a solid bottom and a metal fine screen. You screw it onto the Aeropress instead of the usual cover.

COMES WITH EXTRA VALVE
This even came with an extra valve in it so you can replace it if you need to in the future. I hope it won't be necessary for a long time though.

HOW DOES IT PERFORM?
When I first used this, I was not sure what to expect. I was able to put the right amount of coffee into the tube, put the right amount of water, and the BAM- it stayed put!

Once I waited long enough, I pressed the plunger and it was amazing- the coffee came out nice and strong- in fact PERFECT. Even my fussy daughter liked it.

THE ONLY PROBLEM
The only problem if you want to call it that is that you won't get those perfect little pucks of pressed coffee grounds to pop into the compost. It still comes out, but not as compacted. No big deal, just thought you might want to know.

THE BOTTOM LINE
A PERFECT addition to my Aeropress, this little beauty makes great coffee even better. It keeps the grounds in the water just long enough to make a truly good cup of coffee. I love how it works, and even don't mind losing the satisfaction of popping little pucks of coffee into the compost.

MY RATING
FIVE STARS for this ingenious invention. I really like this and the ability to not have to use paper filters. I love the metal filter and now with this addition, it is PERFECT and very economical too.

Glad This Came With TWO - Broke First One - One of the places my family goes a lot has a Keurig. We don't generally like most of the prepacks of coffee, so we like to take our own. The problem is that some of the containers are rejected, so I wanted one that wouldn't be. Well, glad this came with two.

WHAT ARE THESE LIKE?
This comes with a black and a purple version. The cap opens up and you put the coffee in. You put it in the Keurig and use it, and then normal use would cause it to work. In my case.. *sighs*

WATCH WHAT YOU ARE DOING!
My daughter grabbed the one container I had with me and ran off to make her coffee. She had it in a bag, poured it in and then proceeded to slam the container into the unit, not realizing that you have to put it in ONE DIRECTION ONLY. IF YOU DON'T DO THAT IT WILL POKE A HOLE IN THE UNIT. That is precisely what happened to ours.

DOES THE OTHER ONE WORK?
The other one works as long as the person using it lines up the correct direction BEFORE trying to use it. IT seemed to perform well and the machine did not reject it. So, now I only have one, which is why I wanted two- they could ruin one and mine would stay intact.

CLEAN UP
Clean up is good . I used a tiny scrub brush gently to get the mesh clean.

THE BOTTOM LINE
As long as you use this properly it should work with the machine. If you don't it will poke a hole in it. The coffee works out well if you use it right.

MY RATING
FOUR STARS. I wish these had a more noticeable marking on it to keep this from being used incorrectly. But it is up to use to take our time to use it- as long as the family does not get a hold of it.

Nicer For Single Serving, Otherwise Not As Nice - My family has a little coffee shop in our home. We love to make different styles and types of coffee with a variety of methods. We will do pour over, cold brew, and varieties of other styles of coffee which are not always mainstream. One thing that we love to do is make frothy milk (including non-dairy) to go with it. Let's see how this measures up.

WHAT IS THIS FROTHING JUG LIKE?
This is a HIGH quality jug. It is made from thick stainless steel. It has a wider bottom and narrower top to allow for frothing without splashing of smaller amounts of milk. The handle is not connected at the bottom, allowing for non-transfer of heat like handles which have the bottom attached.

CAN YOU HEAT UP MILK IN THIS?
Although you CAN heat up milk in this jug, it is not a thick bottom, so you have to be careful if you decide to try this.
If you have a very small burner and can warm it up slowly, it can be done. Do I recommend using it on a stove? I think it is too small unless you have a tiny burner available.

HOW MUCH DOES THIS HOLD?
Technically, this jug holds about a cup and a half of liquid. If you really want to froth milk, I would not go above 1/2 cup of milk.

COUNTRY OF ORIGIN?
Italy

HOW WELL DOES THIS WORK?
I have a frother which works best with more milk, but sometimes I just want a personal size of foamy milk. This is what this is good for. When using about 1/3 cup of milk, this will work fairly well. It is not quite as big allowing for as much air as my other frothers, but it does work.

DOES THIS WORK WITH THE NINJA INTELLIGENT COFFEE/TEA MACHINE?
This works nicely with the frother on the side of the Ninja coffee/tea machine. You have to hold it up, press the button, or put something under it to hold it high enough, but it works.

THE BOTTOM LINE
For a single or small dual serving of frothy milk, this jug is helpful. It works decently with a hand frother, Ninja frother, and small steamer/frothers if you have one of those. You can warm up with this on a small burner, but use caution as the bottom is not double thick. This is EXCELLENT quality stainless steel.

MY RATING
Four stars. I find that it takes longer to get a good foam in this jug than it does in a few other larger containers, but it still works. If the bottom section was a bit larger (more bulbous) it might work a bit better. This rating is more like FOUR AND A HALF stars, but I can only do whole stars, hence the four star rating. This is made from beautiful polished stainless.

Great To Take To Coffee Houses But Not My Favorite - I am spoiled. I have had one of the nicest frothers ever. Cafe Casa. It is heavy duty and whips up milk like I have never seen any other hand frothers. I am still open to trying new ones, and this was a decent contender. Let's see how it works.

WHAT IS THIS FROTHER LIKE?
This frother has a cheap plastic base. It feels lightweight and cheap. It has a side switch on it and the whip is heavy duty compared to some that I have tried which are not as nice. There are two AA batteries required to run this and a very lightweight cover on the bottom is easily knocked out of place.

HOW DOES THIS PERFORM?
This frother works well. It does whip nicely but it has a bit of play in the way it spins. The handle is harder to hold and feels very cheap. I do not think it performs as nicely as the Cafe Casa that I have, but because I have to make my own coffee sometimes at some places (diet restrictions) I tend to like to have one I can bring with me when I go. This is a decent one for that if put in a box that will protect it.

ONE PROBLEM
One thing I do not like about this is the bottom. It is more rounded and not flat. That means that it rocks when I stand it upright and easily falls over.

THE BOTTOM LINE
For a less expensive frother that is lightweight and spins well, this is a decent choice. It has a few improvements that would make me like it better, but it is decent.

MY RATING
Three stars. This just is not as solid or nice as Cafe Casa is. It is better than some other frothers I have used anyway, so for that I give it the three- more like three and a half.

The seal is good, too.  There has been no problem with either Keurig machine I have tried in brewing cup after cup with these.  Sample was supplied for evaluation.

Chemex filters are necessary for the best coffee in a Chemex pot - I've been brewing coffee for decades in a Chemex coffee pot.  I also use an assortment of other coffee makers, stove top, table top and electric.  When my supply of the Chemex Bonded Unbleached Pre-folded Square Coffee Filters was depleting, I purchased a reusable hemp filter, thinking that a difference would not be noticeable.  I was so wrong. These bonded paper filters, when moistened with water before filling with freshly ground coffee, produce a coffee that displays the coffee essence without residual solids or added bitterness from the fabric or paper.  The folded square design allows the user to simply pull one free corner to turn a square into a cone that allows for perfect filtering of the hot water as it's gently poured into the coffee carafe.  By slowly adding the water, you can control the drip and my preference is to pour around the outer edge instead of directly into the center of the filter.  In all these years, I have never had a Chemex filter break or slip through and into the carafe, as I've had occur with the hemp filter.  I also like the Chemex round filters and have just ordered a package, so I'll review those once I recall how they compare for me.  I purchased a case of these pre-folded squares some time ago, so I don't remember my impression of the other filters.  If you care about the taste of your coffee, then I absolutely recommend using these with your Chemex.

When done, throw into the compost pile - The Melitta Tea Filters, 40-Count (Pack of 6) is a wonderful way to create tea bags, spice bags, bath salt bags, or whatever you can think of for a secure and economical system.  I generally place loose tea leaves with herbs and spices inside an unbleached bag, then put it into a teapot.  After adding hot water and waiting for the prescribed time period, I easily remove the bag and have a pot of tea with a minimal amount of residue in the brew.  Because a filled bag will remain open, I've also created some gifts by attaching a cotton string and little hand decorated tag to the bags.  For the unsealed bag, be careful that the longer end does not hang over the edge of a cup, mug or teapot too much or you'll run the risk of the hot water being absorbed all the way to the upper edge, saturating it and possibly dripping onto the table top.  This happened to me once because I forgot to retrieve the bag and the teapot sat for about a half hour.  The bags can also be used for mulling spices because they are rather large.  I love them!

Fast cooking but lots to learn. - I was so excited to receive this product. I had been dreaming of an induction range for years and when we updated our kitchen I thought the time was right. It was a little slow in arriving, but that was just a lesson in patience for me. Two days after it arrived we had modified the cabinet space for it so it would fit and I started cooking on it. I was blown away. So fast. Water started to boil in a fairly large pot in less than a minute and was at full boil in three minutes. I love how easy the cooktop is to clean. Matching the pans to the correct diameter cooking area is easy because you have four different sizes to choose from.

The convection oven takes a little getting used to. The waves of heat coming out of the door when I opened it were surprising. And it took forever for it to cool down. I checked and rechecked the controls because it felt like the oven was still on. When I used the oven as a conventional oven I didn't have that problem. Also, a cookie sheet actually blew over the edge of the oven rack when I opened the door using convection. I will probably use the oven in the conventional mode more. It heats up much more quickly than the convection mode too.

I say I will probably use that feature because I don't know what will happen after the service technician gets here next week. I read so many reviews on this range and most were good. There will always be some bad reviews, but I wish I had paid more attention and definitely wish I had purchased it locally so I could return it. Just eight days after it arrived the cooktop failed, in the middle of cooking breakfast. The lights came on and blinked and then shut off. Later the same day it was working fine. Still later the problem had returned. Eight days! And during that time we were out of town for three days so it didn't get used at all while we traveled. So really this very expensive induction range lasted all of 5 days and very few meals. I could not be more disappointed. GE will be sending a service technician out next week, but in the meantime, we have no way to cook. Thank heaven that we have a microwave and a toaster oven that can handle most jobs. I would not recommend this range to anyone. Take the bad reviews to heart and stay away!

UPDATE: While the GE service man was here we discovered that the problem wasn't in the range, but in the plan placement. By moving a pan less than a quarter of an inch it caused the burner to "catch" or "lose" the pan. You need to be VERY aware of pan size and placement on the top of the range. This can result in not being able to use all the burners if you don't have exactly the right size pan to fit. Yesterday I had to use a much bigger pan than I needed because the smallest burner was already taken and the next size burner would not recognize a small pan. This is explained in the owner's manual, but I didn't realize how precise the size of each pan on each burner has to be. I will probably need to buy a few more pots and pans to accommodate the various size burners.

product_name: Ninja Foodi Power Nutri DUO Blender
text:
Looks nice, but poor build quality and reliability - I had high hopes for this ice maker since it makes ice differently than the bullet ice makers. Unfortunately, the build quality is sub-par. It sometimes makes ice just fine (and it is mostly clear ice) but most of the time it fails to freeze properly, resulting in little or no ice production. Additionally, the water pump doesn't work consistently -- sometimes it pumps just fine; other times it just dribbles out the water. All of these issues occurred within the first couple of days of use, so it was not an issue of things wearing out but rather things not working from the get-go.

The external appearance is pretty attractive, with stainless steel cladding all around. But even that has its faults, with small but noticeable blotches that look like rust on the back of the machine. And despite it being under the Frigidaire brand, the user manual is terrible, with limited info and poor English.

Fortunately I was able to return it. I opted not to get a replacement since I had no confidence that the replacement would be any better than the first one. I'll give it two stars since it has the potential to make better quality ice than a bullet ice maker, but the poor reliability makes it a gamble if you get one that works.

Worthwhile, though a bit pricey. - This is an interesting gadget. It allows you to not have to use inverted method to keep from spilling/leaking which is nice. This is a bonus because with inverted you have to fill then put the lid on tight AFTER stirring when you are time-sensitive. If you added too much water or whatever or got grounds stuck or slick hands, this could cause issues.
With this, you can put the lid on FIRST so no stress.

You may need to adjust your grind though. I tried very fine grind and I couldn't even BUDGE the thing! I dropped down to a medium fine grind and this worked decently.
I set the thing about 2 feet off the ground and push so I can use my body weight to shove and get a full extraction.

I do think that this helps generate a stronger shot, vs normal methods (assuming the same temperature and time).

Kind of pricey for what it is, about as much as the entire aeropress itself, but if you like the aeropress, it is worthwhile.

product_name: Ninja Foodi DualZone Air Fryer DZ201
text:
Has a HUGE Safety Issue-Potential Gas Leak - I bought this gas stove less than 5 mo. ago and have run into a very serious design flaw. And thought I have to tell folks about this.

You know how you have to push the knobs in & turn to ignite gas burners- well these knobs need VERY, very little depressing, and they turn very easily too, and a slight turn or bump releases gas without causing the auto ignitor to go on, plus they are high output burners- the combination makes for a GAS LEAK.
It has happened several times to both my partner & myself without noticing until we smell gas. Happens when we're cooking, sometimes happens we reach up to use the microwave above the stove, happened when our dog tried to stove surf-which really freaked us out.
Just yesterday a friend was over and began fixing a cup of coffee on the counter, next to the stove. I guess she may have grazed it with her body as she moved, within a few minutes we all began to smell gas, and we knew just where to check. Sure enough, the damn knob(s).
Some of the knobs seem to protrude out slightly further than the bar/oven handle-so the handle offers no protection of the knobs getting bumped.
Not sure if this problem is limited to our stove, but I would seriously check this stove out in person at a store first before purchasing.

I am not sure where/who to report this potentially deadly problem to, but thought I mention it here first where there is a significant amt of traffic.

I purchased my first machine and within three months the machine started making screeching noises so loud, it woke me up in the middle of the night. I unplugged and let sit for the rest of the night and started anew and shortly thereafter, it started again. The machine was replaced under warranty and the second machine did the same thing....I am now on my third machine and decided to try something to maybe help resolve a potential problem....the company that makes this is evidently not GE, but branded GE...this company told me to descale the machine every month. I have perfectly pure water as I have two filtration systems in my home, but do so anyway and it seems to have helped the situation.
IF the manufacturer can fix this problem so consumers do not have to purchase special descaling solution and spend an hour once a month, it will be the perfect machine.

Big problems, but makes great ice - [[VIDEOID:691c754c7b664c6c99fdabc5f8e7e04b]] The reviews were all positive on this ice maker. It works like a charm.....easy to set up and makes ice in about 20 minutes. Our French door refrigerator has the icemaker in the door and it just does not provide enough ice. This is a perfect solution.
It is a slightly loud appliance, but I set mine up in the laundry room and never hear it working.
PART TWO:
This is an additional review to my original review posted above. After about 8 months the icemaker started making all kinds of weird noises that sounded like creaking and parts rubbing together. It finally was so loud I had to turn it off. I contacted GE about a warranty exchange...luckly I was within four days of the one year expiration. GE does not do their own warranty on this item, so if you have a claim, just be prepared. I had to send photos, videos, recordings of the sounds, file the claim and then wait for the warranty company to get back to me...took some time and finally they advised me they would be shipping a replacement ice maker....not new, but refurbished. What a disappointment...if you read the fine print in the GE warranty it says they "may" send a refurbished model. After waiting almost 40 days I contacted OnPoint (not exactly the best name for their service follow up) and was told..."oh it is due to ship in a day or so."
Sounds like it slipped through the cracks, but who knows...it about a week to ten days more, I got the replacement refurbished model....appears to be ok. Cleaned it as instructed and set back up and so far working. I purchased a second ice maker for my daughter and hopefully it will not have the same problem....the ice maker when it is working is fantastic...I advised her to buy the third party extended warranty just to be sure, which is a good idea for anyone.
Would hate to have to do business with OnPoint again.
PART THREE
This refurbished model arrived, cleaned it as indicated above. However, after about 30 days the icemaker is doing the exact same thing as the other icemaker. I purchased my daughter an Opal unit and hers did exactly the same thing (Amazon replaced it as it happened inside 30 days...thanks Amazon), but after spending over $1,100 on these two icemakers, I am not sure I would recommend buying one unless GE comes to the realization that there is a manufacturing problem. This is the true example of insanity...making the same unit and expecting different results....not happening. I attached a video in case you want to hear what the grinding sounds like....imagine this at 3 AM in the morning!!! Buyer beware.

Researched for 6 months and chose to get the GE. Here are my thoughts. - On GE's implementation of induction:

Please read up on how induction works before buying an induction range. It's nothing like ordinary electric cooktops.

- The induction range is amazingly fast and responsive. With a very thick, 7-ply Demeyere skillet (which normally takes a while to heat up even when empty), the medium-sized burner can boil 1cm water in under 10 seconds. When you turn the heat down, the response is nearly instantaneous -- even with the large heat capacity of this skillet, the water slows its boil in less than 3 seconds, and that's on a smaller burner that does NOT have the "power boil" feature.
I read numbers like this when I was researching induction and they didn't impress me. Really you have to see it in action to realize how much time it saves.

- I heard complaints about "buzzing" on GE's previous model of induction range. Some people even returned it for that reason. Here's the skinny:
> When on low heat, below 5 1/2, the range "clicks" as it (presumably) turns on and off. The click is about as loud as lightly tapping a Bic pen against the counter. I don't find it distracting or annoying.
> When on high heat, you can hear an "electric" sound. It doesn't sound much different to me than the sound I hear when I turn on high-powered standard electric cooktops, except that the electric cooktop is usually silent after a short time while the induction range keeps making the sound.
> The sound itself isn't loud, and I wouldn't describe it as "buzzing." What it sounds like is an electric transformer. I'm sure you've heard one before -- they are the giant grey cylinders on some power lines. You hear the same sound near high-tension power lines. It's exactly like that (in fact, the sound is probably coming from a transformer in the range), but it isn't as loud. I don't have sound measuring equipment, but i'd say that it's noticeable in the kitchen but not in the adjacent dining room except for when everything else is silent.

- While arguably the greatest benefit of induction is that the range can vary temperature with even greater control than gas, this range does do the "on/off" switching at low settings (those below 5 1/2 out of 10). In practice, this hasn't reduced temperature or caused hot-spotting like it does on a electric glasstop, but it might when using very cheap, thin pans.

- GE's range detects pan sizes and won't turn on if the pan is off the burner or is too small. This prevents accidentally heating a stainless steel wedding ring, for example, or a steel spatula. It also prevents you from using small pans on large burners, though this isn't a problem in practice... Except for the very large burner. We don't have any pans large enough for it to turn on. Not a problem though, the other burners work perfectly (Update: We now have a few pans large enough for the big burner and they heat up even faster than the other burners. According to the C-Net review, the large burner is the fastest-heating burner they have ever seen by quite a bit).

- On changing pans: This was surprisingly cheap and easy for us. T-Fal has a whole set for under $99 (as if this writing) that works perfectly on induction. See http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B000GWL8T4/
We have some "nicer" pans, like Demeyere, but they don't seem to work any better and cost a fortune (A single skillet, while clearly well made, cost three times more than the entire T-Fal set!).
You still want to do your homework on induction. If magnets do not stick to your pan's bottom, they probably won't work at all. The pan's bottom surface must made from one of: Most types of stainless steel, mild (ordinary) steel, or cast iron. Aluminum pans work only if the bottom is made from one of the above--all aluminum or all-copper pans do not work at all

On the oven:

- If you are worried about changing all your cook times with a new oven, note that you can adjust the thermostat of the GE to match your old range.

- The racks don't seem to be especially well designed/manufactured, The slide-out rack was very difficult to fit into the oven, even with significant force, and scratched its sides a little. The other racks seem to be a little too wide as well and are difficult to pull out. They also make a rather unpleasant grinding sound as they slide along the oven walls.
(UPDATE December 2015: The racks have "set" and are now easy to slide in/out. The aforementioned scratches were not deep and did not penetrate the protective powder coating of the inside of the range).

- The oven doesn't preheat especially quickly, but it does tell you its temperature in realtime as it heats.

- We haven't used it much at all and probably need more practice, but we did try cooking two pizzas at the same time with convection mode. The top pizza did not cook as much as the one on the bottom, though it did have slightly thicker toppings. Technically for 2 pizzas, we are suppose to position two racks equidistant from the center of the oven. We kept the 3-rack configuration because, as mentioned above, shifting racks is awkward.

- The oven's window is larger than almost any oven's I've ever seen. It seems larger in person than the photo makes it look.

- The oven light is near the door of the oven, so I guess it's easy to replace. I've never had to replace an oven light, though, so this is speculation.

- The convection fan is audible but not loud, high-pitched, or distracting. It sounds more like white noise.

On the controls:
- The entire control surface is capacitive touch glass, like on a smart phone. It works very well and rarely misses a tap. It is a little sensitive, though. When leaning over the oven (which admittedly is not done often), it sometimes thinks the "set timer" button has been pushed. The controls do have a lock-out feature to prevent this from happening and presumably to prevent kids from playing with it.

- Range settings start at 5 of 10 when you turn them on. You then change the power with +/- buttons that shift the value by 1/2 with each press. Thus, it's slower to set the a burner to "2" than it is with old-style knobs. This problem is partly resolved by special buttons. One burner has a "simmer" button, which immediately sets power to 2. Another has a "Melt", which sets it to 1. The large burner has a "Power broil" button, which presumably sets it above 10/10 for a time. (Update: It just sets it to 10/10)
In practice, changing settings is still pretty fast. The GE Monogram cooktop has a slide-to-set feature which I like even better, but it hasn't trickled down to the mid-range line.

- Overall I am happy with the design of the controls. They feel intuitive, and give you hints as for what to do next. For example, when you set the timer and enter the time, the "Set timer" button blinks to remind you to tell the oven that you are ready to start the timer. There are many small touches like that which tell me that the GE engineers spend some time thinking about how to make their range easy to use.

UPDATE: After two years, we have had zero problems with the unit. The glass surface still looks good, with only some very minor scratches.

text:
My wife loved her Dyson V11 Torque Drive Cordless Vacuum; I thought it was flawed and overpriced, but neither of these is the issue.

Chrome AI displays "Dyson customer service reviews are overwhelmingly negative" and I concur. This is seriously damning for an over-priced product. Here's my experience...

Three years after purchase the motor failed on ours. That's not a long life at their price. But fine... I can diagnose and repair it by replacing the small motor unit. Oh, but I cannot get parts through Dyson: their department only sells larger units than just the motor... except they don't even stock their $230 replacement head-unit.

Maybe I can find a compatible (inevitably Chinese) motor. But... with such a "compatible motor" in hand, I stripped the vac' and found they'd switched the connectors on the motor from 2 to 5 prongs - and none of the scores of motor assemblies I've examined have the 5-prong connector.

Looking at the Dyson web-site I've failed to find a way to ask them the obvious questions. They want to sell you things, or answer shipping details for their products.... but questions about parts or problems aren't covered. I'm inferring that in their opinion it's a $500 (now $600) paperweight, and I should just buy another.

This is not a company that courts repeat buyers.

product_name: Shark Navigator Lift-Away ADV Upright Vacuum
text:
I have had this Dyson since 2020, so six years now . I love it . It’s my favorite tool to clean with, although mine doesn’t have the light attachment . It does a hell of a job picking up most things from my hardwood and composite tile floors - cat hair , my hair (very long ) , kids hair (very thick ) , dirt , crumbs, almost everything. I’d even say be careful before using it - pick up the things you DON’T want to get stuck in it ( toys, hard plastics, screws, coins, other metal, etc) since the suction is really good in this thing . However …

The first photo is me pointing to the issue- it is a plastic piece that has been broken off of the top of the battery unit today , which I have detached from the vacuum using the world’s smallest damn screwdriver in order to assess the damage . Not sure how this break (inside) the vacuum has happened .
The second photo is the piece that has broken off , which decided to split into two pieces . This tiny ass piece of plastic is what is supposed to keep the battery mechanism in place in order to apply the necessary pressure to activate the trigger , in order to use the vacuum. THIS is the piece preventing me from using the vacuum, rendering it totally inoperable. For over $600 there should be a lot less easily breakable cheap ass plastic in this thing . I’m not sure if customer service can even help with this . And of course, the warranty has been expired… I would recommend getting the warranty if you have kids or irresponsible adults using it - it’s cheaper than replacing the whole thing .

Pros : very , VERY good suction. Battery has decent longevity on medium mode which I use most often. Doesn’t take long to fully charge (depending on usage- I will use mine for 20-30 minutes at a time and will charge fully within an hour ). Likely won’t need to replace this vacuum for years with proper maintenance and upkeep. Since it’s cordless, you can literally use it anywhere and nearly every surface - it’s great on all of the many stairs in my home . I use it on my hardwood floors without issues. I don’t have carpet anymore but it worked great on the carpets I used to have (low pile). I’ve used it to clean out my car as well without issues . I use it to vacuum my couch too , and the attachments are useful , with some getting a lot more use than others. The display screen on the top is useful in telling you what percentage your battery is on , what mode you’re in , if something is stuck and where in your vacuum the obstruction can be located and when to clean the filter.

Cons: I have had to replace some parts for this over the years - the trigger (I did buy a new one that came with a bonus trigger and all of the tools you would need to replace it), tool attachment clip , filters (to be expected) and another set of tools (brushes, pipe cleaner style tools ) to clean out the vacuum drum when it inevitably gets clogged and dirty. I feel like cleaning tools for the unit should have been included. Although cordless, it is kind of heavy for one handed use , if you are a young kid, older person, have weakened wrists , carpal tunnel or nerve issues in your hands . The high suction mode can be really loud . You HAVE to buy a wall mount or stand or something because it doesn’t stand on its own, and you DON’T want this expensive ass vacuum just on the floor to be stepped on , kicked or otherwise damaged . A wall mount or free standing mount (sold separately) will help to house your attachments and vacuum unit as well, since the attachment clip included can only hold two of the vacuum attachments.
I would not recommend letting kids use this without supervision or being told how to use it , properly empty it and safely store it after each use . Don’t drop this thing ! It seems delicate with all the plastic parts. Don’t vacuum water with it or try to use it while the filter is wet or it will be FUBAR . The included filter , if you wash it (which you absolutely should after awhile - the vacuum will tell you on the display when you should wash it ) needs to dry for a full 24 hours before you can use it again , so I’d recommend buying replacement filters so you can use your vacuum whenever you need it .

Basically: if you have $600 to blow (plus more for a stand , cleaning kit and replacement parts) and you like your floors clean, this is somewhat worth the money for the suction power alone , but it’s very delicate for being so expensive .

text:
I received this Ninja blender (Model BL660) as a birthday gift in July 2012, just a few months after it was released. Fourteen years later, it's still one of my favorite kitchen appliances.

This countertop powerhouse has survived five moves and years of regular use. Some months it's used every single day, while other times it sits untouched for weeks. No matter how often I use it, it has never let me down.

Most of the time I make frozen fruit protein smoothies using the single-serve cups. They're the perfect size, although I have a habit of overfilling them. Even after years of blending frozen fruit and ice, the original blades are still sharp, and the motor feels just as powerful as the day I got it.

During the summer of 2025, I made smoothies every day for five different people. Of course, everyone wanted a different flavor, so the single-serve cups got a serious workout instead of the large pitcher. The blender handled the constant use without missing a beat.

For frozen drinks, it crushes ice well. The texture is more like a QT Freezoni or a homemade frozen drink than a perfectly smooth gas station slushie.

This blender is definitely on the louder side, but I think that's expected for a machine with this much power.

Everything is still original -- including the pitcher, cups, lids, blades, and motor. Nothing has cracked, leaked, or needed to be replaced. The only time I've ever had liquid escape is when I've overfilled a cup, opened it, and then blended again without wiping the rim first. That's user error, not a design flaw.

I always wash mine by hand rather than using the dishwasher. My usual routine is to fill the cup with cold water and blend it for a few seconds immediately after making a smoothie, then finish washing it by hand. That quick rinse makes cleanup easy, and avoiding the dishwasher may be one reason the plastic has held up so well.

I only have two minor notes. First, the original travel cup lids have an open drinking spout, so they aren't leakproof enough for tossing into a bag. Since I sometimes commute to work by bicycle, I transfer my smoothie into another container. It appears the newer models include improved lids, which is a nice upgrade. Second, I occasionally don't line up the blade assembly correctly on the base and have to remove it and try again. That's likely more of a "me" issue than a problem with the blender.

I recently recommended this blender to a coworker, when asked what I use for my smoothies. I wasn't even sure Ninja still made this model, so I looked it up -- and when I found it was still available, I knew I had to leave a review.

After 14 years, five moves, countless smoothies, and thousands of blends, I can honestly say this has been one of the best kitchen appliances I've ever owned.

text:
While the response was fast and hassle free, there were 2 UNwelcome surprises during this insurance coverage.
(I DON'T like surprises that do NOT work in my favor during any kind of financial transaction.)
The first surprise was shortly after getting the coverage - I found out that this "Protection Plan" does NOT protect anything until AFTER the manufacturer's warranty EXPIRES. So, in fact, except for some limited situations, the "3 Year" Plan is really ONLY 2-years long.
The second surprise came when I made a claim. Again, while the claim was processed quickly and easily, Asurion UNilaterally decided HOW they were going to pay out the claim, without any prior discussion with me. Asurion decided that they were going to pay the claim using an Amazon Gift Card! I very carefully checked the "Terms & Conditions" document; there is NO mention of HOW claims will be paid.
When I contacted Asurion to complain about this, the nice, knowledgeable agent I spoke with basically said, that's what we (Asurion) do, that's what we did...so, end of story. The agent also erroneously told me that an Amazon Gift Card can be used for 'anything'. I know from personal experience that that is NOT the actual truth. I have in the past "found out the hard way" that Amazon can just decide that certain products for sale on its marketplace are NOT 'eligible' to be paid for with a gift card: the customer doesn't find this out until it's time to pay for an order, and the Gift Card does NOT appear as a form of payment! (And, yes, I called Amazon and found out that this is indeed can happen!)

to reap, while purchasing the Protection Plan and making a claim within it was 'quick and easy', overall, I was unhappy with the coverage of the Plan.
So, I will NOT be purchasing a Protection Plan from Asurion again, until these unfair aspects are corrected.
